topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAD862 - BIO INSPIRED OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Swarm Intelligence - Biological Foundations of Swarm Intelligence, Swarm Intelligence in Optimization, Ant Colonies - Ant Foraging Behaviour, Towards Artificial Ants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Swarm Intelligence (SI) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** is a sub-field of Computational Intelligence and Bio-Inspired Optimization that deals with the design of intelligent **multi-agent systems** taking inspiration from the **collective, decentralized, and self-organized behavior** of social insect colonies and other animal societies (e.g., ant colonies, bee swarms, bird flocks, fish schools).

In the context of KTU's optimization framework, SI is specifically used as a **meta-heuristic** (a high-level problem-independent strategy) to find near-optimal solutions to complex combinatorial and numerical optimization problems by exploiting the **emergent intelligence** that arises from local interactions among simple agents.

**Core Canonical Properties** (as per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology):
- **Proximity Principle** — agents must be able to evaluate simple local computations.
- **Quality Principle** — agents must respond to quality factors in the environment.
- **Principle of Diverse Response** — the colony should not commit to a single narrow path of activity.
- **Stability Principle** — the colony should not change behaviour with every fluctuation in the environment.
- **Adaptability Principle** — the colony must be able to shift to a better behaviour when it becomes worth the computational cost.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cricket Stadium Analogy** — Imagine a packed cricket stadium (e.g., a KTU fest day in Trivandrum). No single spectator is "smart" enough to organize the famous Mexican Wave. Yet, when individuals observe 2-3 neighbours standing and waving, they follow suit. The wave propagates around the entire stadium. **No central conductor exists**, yet a beautiful, intelligent, large-scale pattern emerges from purely local, neighbour-based rules. This is Swarm Intelligence.

In optimization terms: each agent is a *stupid* rule-follower, but together the swarm converges to a *smart* optimal solution.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics in SI

- **Population size (N)** — typically 20 to 100 agents.
- **Iteration budget (T)** — 500 to 5000 epochs.
- **Convergence ratio** — fraction of runs that find the global optimum.
- **Stagnation rate** — measure of premature convergence (a critical pitfall).
- **Pheromone evaporation coefficient (ρ)** — **0 < ρ < 1**, standard value **ρ = 0.5**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stigmergy** — the *single most important concept* in Module 2. It is a mechanism of **indirect coordination** between agents (or agents-to-environment) where the trace left in the environment by one agent stimulates the subsequent actions of other agents. Real ants communicate purely through stigmergy via **pheromone trails**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biological Foundations of Swarm Intelligence

Swarm Intelligence is rooted in the empirical observation of **eusocial insects** (ants, bees, termites, wasps) that exhibit the four pillars of swarming behavior:

**Pillar 1: Self-Organization (SO)**
- No external director; the macroscopic order emerges from microscopic chaos.
- Achieved through: *positive feedback* (reinforcement), *negative feedback* (evaporation), *amplification of fluctuations* (random exploration), and *multiple interactions* (population density).

**Pillar 2: Stigmergy**
- Two types:
  - **Sematectonic stigmergy** — physical environment changes (e.g., nest construction).
  - **Sign-based stigmergy** — chemical/light signals (e.g., pheromone deposition).

**Pillar 3: Division of Labour**
- Task allocation based on internal thresholds (age, physiology) — gives swarm **resilience** and **scalability**.

**Pillar 4: Emergence**
- The whole is greater than the sum of its parts (e.g., the famous "ant mill" or death spiral shows *negative emergence*).

### 2.2 Swarm Intelligence in Optimization

To convert a biological swarm into a **problem-solver**, three abstractions are made:

1. **Discretization** — continuous real space is mapped to a finite search space (graph).
2. **Probabilistic Decision Rule** — instead of deterministic movement, agents use probability distributions over candidate moves.
3. **Heuristic Guidance (η)** — domain-specific knowledge is injected (e.g., distance in TSP), acting like "memory" the biological agents don't have.

**Generic SI Optimization Loop:**
- **Step 1** — Initialize agent population with random solutions and algorithm parameters.
- **Step 2** — Evaluate the fitness of each agent.
- **Step 3** — Apply the **transition rule** to generate new candidate solutions.
- **Step 4** — Apply the **updating rule** (e.g., pheromone update in ACO, velocity update in PSO).
- **Step 5** — Check termination; if not converged, return to Step 2.

### 2.3 Ant Colonies — Ant Foraging Behaviour

Real ants are nearly blind; they solve the *shortest path problem* in nature purely through chemistry. The **double-bridge experiment** (Goss, Aron, Deneubourg 1989) is canonical KTU content.

**Experiment:** Two bridges of unequal length connect a nest to a food source. Initially, ants pick either bridge randomly (50:50). Because the shorter bridge has shorter travel time, more ants return over it and deposit pheromone. Eventually, the colony converges to the shorter bridge.

**The Foraging Algorithm in Nature:**
- **Rule 1 — Exploration:** An ant leaving the nest has *no* trail to follow, so it chooses randomly.
- **Rule 2 — Exploitation:** An ant finding a pheromone trail tends to follow it with probability proportional to the trail intensity.
- **Rule 3 — Pheromone Evaporation:** Trails evaporate exponentially (first-order kinetics), preventing unlimited amplification and supporting exploration of new routes.
- **Rule 4 — Pheromone Deposition:** On returning successfully, the ant deposits Δτ pheromone per unit length of the path it just traversed.

### 2.4 Towards Artificial Ants

Artificial ants extend biological ants with three engineered capabilities:
- **Memory** — artificial ants remember visited nodes to avoid returning (forbidden list).
- **Discrete time + synchronous updates** — unlike continuous biological foraging.
- **Problem-specific heuristics** — to bias the search toward promising regions.

This leads to the **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** meta-heuristic formalized by **Marco Dorigo (1992, PhD Thesis)**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|-------------------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_{i}^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | Probability ant $k$ at node $i$ moves to node $j$ | $\sum P = 1$ |
| 2 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta \tau_{ij}(t)$ | Pheromone evaporation + update | $0 < \rho < 1$, typically **0.5** |
| 3 | $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t) = \dfrac{Q}{L_{k}(t)}$ | Pheromone deposited by ant $k$ on edge $(i,j)$ | $Q$ = constant, $L_k$ = tour length |
| 4 | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | Heuristic desirability (visibility) of edge $(i,j)$ | $d_{ij}$ = distance |
| 5 | $\tau_{ij}(t) = \tau_{0} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot L_{nn}}$ | Initial pheromone value | $n$ = number of nodes |
| 6 | $\alpha$ | Pheromone influence weight | $\alpha \in [1, 5]$ |
| 7 | $\beta$ | Heuristic influence weight | $\beta \in [2, 5]$ |
| 8 | $m$ | Number of artificial ants | $m \approx n$ (one ant per node) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The formula in row 1 is the *transition rule* of ACO, the *exact* formula KTU will ask you to derive or interpret. Memorize its two parts: $\tau^{\alpha}$ is **memory** (swarm knowledge), and $\eta^{\beta}$ is **vision** (greedy heuristic).

**Real-world utility:** ACO and its variants are deployed for vehicle routing (Amazon logistics), network packet routing, robotic path planning, and protein folding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pheromone Update Equation

The pheromone on edge $(i,j)$ at the end of iteration $t$ depends on two physical phenomena: **evaporation** and **deposition**. We derive this from first principles.

**Step 1 — Continuous Evaporation as a Differential Equation**

The pheromone evaporates at a rate proportional to its current concentration. Let $\rho$ be the evaporation coefficient. Then:

$$\frac{d\tau}{dt} = -\rho \cdot \tau(t)$$

**Step 2 — Solve the Linear ODE**

Separating variables and integrating over one time step $\Delta t = 1$:

$$\int_{\tau(t)}^{\tau(t+1)} \frac{1}{\tau} \, d\tau = -\rho \int_{t}^{t+1} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\tau(t+1)}{\tau(t)}\right) = -\rho$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to obtain the decay factor**

$$\frac{\tau(t+1)}{\tau(t)} = e^{-\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the linearization used in ACO**

For small $\rho$, use the first-order Taylor expansion $e^{-\rho} \approx 1 - \rho$. This is the standard simplification:

$$\tau_{\text{after evap}}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau(t)$$

**Step 5 — Add the deposition term**

Let $m$ ants traverse the edge in iteration $t$. Each ant $k$ deposits $\Delta \tau^{k}$. The total deposition is:

$$\Delta \tau_{ij}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

**Step 6 — Combine Evaporation and Deposition**

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta \tau_{ij}(t)\;}$$

**Logic:** The $(1-\rho)$ factor gives **exponential decay** (negative feedback), while $\Delta \tau$ gives **reinforcement** (positive feedback). This balance is the mathematical heart of self-organization.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Probabilistic Transition Rule

**Step 1 — Start from the conditioning principle**

An ant at node $i$ observes $N_i^k$ unvisited candidate nodes. It should choose node $j$ with probability proportional to the *combined strength* of the pheromone trail and the heuristic desirability.

**Step 2 — Define the "attractiveness" of edge $(i,j)$**

$$A_{ij} = [\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the normalization condition**

For the ant's choice to be a valid probability distribution, we need $\sum_{j \in N_i^k} P_{ij}^{k} = 1$. Therefore, divide by the sum of all attractivesses:

$$P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_{i}^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$$

**Step 4 — Special case: $\alpha = 0$**

The pheromone component vanishes. We get a *purely greedy* heuristic, equivalent to the nearest-neighbour algorithm.

**Step 5 — Special case: $\beta = 0$**

The heuristic vanishes. We get a *purely stochastic* selection proportional to pheromone — the algorithm becomes a slow reinforcement learner.

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

The exponents $\alpha$ and $\beta$ form a *trade-off dial*:
- Large $\alpha$ → the swarm **exploits** past knowledge (risk of premature convergence).
- Large $\beta$ → the swarm **explores** greedy solutions (risk of local optima).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Real Ants vs. Artificial Ants

| # | Property | Real Ants | Artificial Ants |
|---|----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Memory | None (only current sense) | Tabu list of visited nodes |
| 2 | Time | Continuous, asynchronous | Discrete iterations, synchronous |
| 3 | Pheromone | Volatile organic molecules | Numeric value in a table |
| 4 | Pheromone range | Local (decimetres) | Global (the whole graph) |
| 5 | Heuristic $\eta$ | None | Injected by designer |
| 6 | Determinism | Stochastic + biased | Probabilistic + heuristic |
| 7 | Backtracking | Allowed (U-turns) | Forbidden in tabu list |
| 8 | Computational cost | Biological energy | CPU cycles |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Ant Foraging on a 4-City TSP

**Problem:** Four cities A, B, C, D with distance matrix:

$$D = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 2 & 7 & 3 \\ 2 & \infty & 4 & 6 \\ 7 & 4 & \infty & 5 \\ 3 & 6 & 5 & \infty \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Compute initial heuristic $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$**

$$\eta_{AB} = 1/2 = 0.50, \quad \eta_{AC} = 1/7 \approx 0.143, \quad \eta_{AD} = 1/3 \approx 0.333$$

**Step 2 — Assume initial pheromone $\tau_0 = 0.5$ on all edges; let $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$**

**Step 3 — Ant at A computes attractiveness for each neighbour**

For edge AB: $A_{AB} = (0.5)^{1} \cdot (0.50)^{2} = 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.1250$

For edge AC: $A_{AC} = (0.5)^{1} \cdot (0.143)^{2} = 0.5 \times 0.0204 = 0.0102$

For edge AD: $A_{AD} = (0.5)^{1} \cdot (0.333)^{2} = 0.5 \times 0.111 = 0.0555$

**Step 4 — Normalize to get probabilities**

$$\sum A = 0.1250 + 0.0102 + 0.0555 = 0.1907$$

$$P_{AB} = \frac{0.1250}{0.1907} \approx 0.656 \quad (65.6\%)$$

$$P_{AC} = \frac{0.0102}{0.1907} \approx 0.053 \quad (5.3\%)$$

$$P_{AD} = \frac{0.0555}{0.1907} \approx 0.291 \quad (29.1\%)$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

The ant at A has a **65.6% chance** of going to B. This is the shortest edge, and the swarm will rapidly amplify the pheromone on edge AB. After 10–20 iterations, the ant colony will almost deterministically pick the shortest tour, e.g., **A → B → C → D → A** with $L = 2 + 4 + 5 + 3 = 14$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SI-Based Optimization

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional flow of a Swarm Intelligence (specifically, ACO) solver. It is engineered as a block-level schematic to render reliably across all viewers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Agent Population] --> B[Initialize Memory Structures]
    B --> C[Initialize Pheromone Matrix tau_0]
    C --> D[Iteration Counter t = 1]
    D --> E[Position Each Ant on Starting Node]
    E --> F[Compute Heuristic eta = 1 / d]
    F --> G[Apply Probabilistic Transition Rule]
    G --> H{All Nodes Visited?}
    H -- No --> G
    H -- Yes --> I[Compute Tour Length L_k]
    I --> J[Identify Best Ant in Iteration]
    J --> K[Update Best Global Solution]
    K --> L[Apply Pheromone Evaporation]
    L --> M[Apply Pheromone Deposition]
    M --> N{t less than T_max?}
    N -- No --> O[Output Best Solution]
    N -- Yes --> P[Increment t]
    P --> E
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Mapping: Biological Ants to Artificial Ants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Bio["Biological Ants in Nature"]
        B1[Real Foraging Ants]
        B2[Chemical Pheromone Trail]
        B3[Local Sensing of Trail]
        B4[Probabilistic Path Choice]
        B5[Evaporation over Time]
    end
    subgraph Engg["Artificial Ants in ACO"]
        E1[Software Agents]
        E2[Numeric Pheromone Table tau]
        E3[Read Pheromone Values]
        E4[Formula based Choice P_ij]
        E5[Decay Factor 1 minus rho]
    end
    B1 -- modeled as --> E1
    B2 -- mapped to --> E2
    B3 -- mapped to --> E3
    B4 -- mapped to --> E4
    B5 -- mapped to --> E5
```

### 4.3 Emergence Diagram — From Local Rules to Global Optimum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Local Rule 1: Pheromone Deposit] --> E
    L2[Local Rule 2: Pheromone Evaporation] --> E
    L3[Local Rule 3: Probabilistic Following] --> E
    L4[Local Rule 4: Threshold Based Response] --> E
    E{Emergent Global Behavior}
    E --> G1[Shortest Path Discovery]
    E --> G2[Cemetery Organization]
    E --> G3[Task Allocation]
    E --> G4[Bridge Formation]
    E --> G5[Collective Transport]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Double-Bridge Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Nest] -- Short Bridge length 4 --> F[Food Source]
    N -- Long Bridge length 8 --> F
    F -- Pheromone Returns --> N
```

**Interpretation:** Although both branches start with equal probability, the shorter branch receives pheromone reinforcement first. This positive feedback loop rapidly amplifies the short bridge, and the swarm converges to it within 4–5 ant cycles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define Swarm Intelligence. List the four canonical properties of Swarm Intelligence as defined by Millonas (1994).** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Swarm Intelligence (SI) is a property of systems of non-intelligent agents exhibiting **collectively intelligent behavior** through local interactions, self-organization, and stigmergy, with the goal of solving complex optimization problems.

**Four Canonical Properties (Millonas 1994):**
1. **Proximity Principle** — agents must be capable of simple local computation.
2. **Quality Principle** — agents must respond to environmental quality factors.
3. **Principle of Diverse Response** — the system should distribute resources across many activities.
4. **Stability Principle** — the system should not oscillate erratically.
5. **Adaptability Principle** — the system must be able to shift to a better behavior when needed.

> *(Note: Millonas proposed 5 properties, not 4; examiners often accept either. Mentioning all five is the safe KTU move.)*

**Marks Distribution:** Definition 1 mark + 4 properties × 0.5 = 1 mark + list format 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is Stigmergy? Explain how it is used by real ants during foraging.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Stigmergy** is a form of **indirect communication** among agents mediated by modifications of the environment.

**Application in ant foraging:**
- An ant returning to the nest from a food source deposits a chemical called **pheromone** along the path traversed.
- This pheromone acts as a "sign" (sign-based stigmergy) that **biases other ants** to follow the same trail.
- The strength of the trail is proportional to the number of ants that previously used it.
- **Pheromone evaporation** introduces a forgetting mechanism, so shorter paths (re-traversed more often per unit time) accumulate pheromone faster and dominate.
- Result: **emergent** shortest-path selection without any central coordination.

**Marks Distribution:** Stigmergy definition 1 mark + 4 points of explanation 2 marks.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the **biological foundations of Swarm Intelligence**. Discuss **self-organization, stigmergy, division of labour, and emergence** in the context of real ant colonies. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **double-bridge experiment** by Deneubourg et al. (1989). Show mathematically how the probability of choosing a branch evolves with time when $n_s$ and $n_l$ ants have used the short and long bridges respectively. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**1. Biological Foundations of SI (1 mark):**
SI draws inspiration from **eusocial insect colonies** (ants, bees, wasps, termites) that solve complex environmental problems without any leader or blueprint.

**2. Self-Organization (2 marks):**
- Defined as the mechanism where **macroscopic order emerges from microscopic local interactions** without external control.
- Achieved via 4 ingredients:
  - *Positive feedback* (e.g., pheromone deposit on short paths).
  - *Negative feedback* (e.g., pheromone evaporation).
  - *Amplification of fluctuations* (e.g., random initial exploration).
  - *Multiple interactions* (population size must be sufficient).
- Example: A single ant has no notion of "nest architecture," but together they build ventilation, brood chambers, and cemeteries.

**3. Stigmergy (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Indirect coordination via environmental modification.
- Two forms: *sematectonic* (e.g., nest-building) and *sign-based* (e.g., pheromones).
- Allows *decoupled* communication in time and space — the depositing ant and the following ant need not meet.

**4. Division of Labour (1 mark):**
- Tasks are dynamically allocated based on individual thresholds (age, hunger, caste).
- Provides **resilience**: loss of one worker does not collapse the system.

**5. Emergence (1 mark):**
- The *whole* displays properties absent in any *part*. Ant foraging, nest thermoregulation, and swarm migration are all emergent phenomena.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**1. Experiment Setup (2 marks):**
Place the ant nest and a food source connected by **two bridges** of lengths $L_s$ (short) and $L_l$ (long), with $L_s < L_l$. Ants leaving the nest encounter a binary choice at the bifurcation point. The diagram:

```
   NEST ────── short bridge (Ls) ──────► FOOD
     │
     │
     └──── long bridge (Ll) ──────────────►
```

**2. Initial Behaviour (1 mark):**
Without any pheromone, each ant chooses each branch with probability 0.5. Due to stochastic fluctuations, one branch will receive a slightly higher ant count by chance.

**3. Probability Model (4 marks):**
Let $A_s$ and $A_l$ be the amount of pheromone on the short and long bridge. An ant chooses the short bridge with probability:

$$P_s = \frac{(A_s)^a}{(A_s)^a + (A_l)^a}$$

where $a$ is a non-linearity exponent (experimentally, $a \approx 2$).

Now, the number of ants that have used the short bridge by time $t$ is $n_s(t)$, and the long bridge is $n_l(t)$. The rate of pheromone accumulation is **inversely proportional to path length**, because ants on the short bridge complete the round-trip faster. Therefore:

$$A_s(t) = k \cdot \frac{n_s(t)}{L_s} \quad \text{and} \quad A_l(t) = k \cdot \frac{n_l(t)}{L_l}$$

**4. Convergence Argument:**
Substitute and rearrange:

$$P_s = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{n_l \cdot L_s}{n_s \cdot L_l}\right)^{a}}$$

A simple fixed-point analysis shows that the equilibria $P_s = 0$ and $P_s = 1$ are stable, while $P_s = 0.5$ is unstable. Therefore, *any* initial fluctuation will amplify and the colony converges to using the short bridge with probability → 1.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2]**
**(a)** Define the **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** meta-heuristic. With suitable equations, describe the **pheromone update rule** and the **probabilistic transition rule** of an artificial ant system. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Distinguish clearly between **real ants and artificial ants** in **at least six** dimensions. State **any four real-world applications** of ACO. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**1. Definition of ACO (1 mark):**
**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a population-based **meta-heuristic** introduced by **Marco Dorigo (1992)** in his PhD thesis. It solves combinatorial optimization problems by simulating artificial ants that build solutions guided by synthetic pheromone trails and problem-specific heuristics.

**2. The Three Pillars of ACO Algorithm (1 mark):**
- **Solution construction** — each ant builds a tour.
- **Pheromone update** — global memory refinement.
- **Daemon actions** — optional local search improvement.

**3. Pheromone Update Rule (3 marks):**
After all $m$ ants complete their tours in iteration $t$:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

where $\rho \in (0,1)$ is the **evaporation coefficient** and $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$ is the pheromone deposited by ant $k$ on edge $(i,j)$:

$$\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k(t)}, & \text{if ant } k \text{ traversed edge } (i,j) \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Here $Q$ is a constant and $L_k$ is the total tour length of ant $k$.

**4. Probabilistic Transition Rule (2 marks):**
At node $i$, ant $k$ moves to the next node $j$ with probability:

$$P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_{i}^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}, & j \in N_{i}^{k} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $N_{i}^{k}$ is the set of unvisited nodes for ant $k$ at node $i$, $\alpha$ controls the influence of past experience, and $\beta$ controls the influence of the greedy heuristic $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**1. Distinction Table: Real Ants vs. Artificial Ants (4 marks):**

| # | Dimension | Real Ants | Artificial Ants |
|---|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Memory | None — only current chemo-sensing | **Tabu list** of visited nodes |
| 2 | Time model | Continuous, asynchronous | Discrete **iterations**, synchronous |
| 3 | Pheromone persistence | Volatile organic molecules, ~minutes | **Numeric value** in matrix, indefinite |
| 4 | Spatial scope | **Local** (few cm radius) | **Global** (entire graph) |
| 5 | Heuristic | None (blind) | Injected $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ |
| 6 | Backtracking | Physically possible | **Forbidden** by tabu list |
| 7 | Population size | Hundreds to millions | 20–100 (computational limit) |

**2. Real-World Applications of ACO (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
- **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — Dorigo's original benchmark; finds near-optimal city tours.
- **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** — used by Amazon, FedEx, DHL for last-mile delivery optimization.
- **Network Routing (AntNet)** — packet routing in telecommunication networks.
- **Job-Shop Scheduling** — assigns manufacturing jobs to machines minimizing makespan.
- *(Bonus — 1 extra application)* — **Protein Folding / Bioinformatics** and **Robotic Path Planning**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forget to specify the time-step normalization** (e.g., $\Delta t = 1$) in the pheromone derivation — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confuse evaporation coefficient** $\rho$ with the **heuristic coefficient** $\beta$ — very common; double-check your notation.
> 3. **Miss the tabu list** in the distinction table — examiners specifically test this engineering addition.
> 4. **Omit the denominator** in the transition rule formula — partial-mark trap. Write the **full** normalized equation.
> 5. **Do not say "pheromone is a real chemical in ACO"** — it is a **numeric abstraction** stored in a computer memory. This conceptual error costs 1 full mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Swarm Intelligence** = *collective intelligence* from *simple local rules* + *decentralized control* + *stigmergy*.
- **Eusocial insects** (ants, bees) are the canonical biological inspiration; their behavior is *not* centrally orchestrated.
- **Four pillars of biological SI**: Self-organization, Stigmergy, Division of labour, Emergence.
- **Millonas's 5 principles**: Proximity, Quality, Diverse Response, Stability, Adaptability.
- **Stigmergy** is **indirect, environment-mediated communication** — the *central* concept of Module 2.
- **Real ant foraging** uses **chemical pheromone** that **evaporates over time**, enabling shortest-path discovery without any map or memory.
- **Double-bridge experiment (Deneubourg 1989)** — empirical proof that real ants solve shortest-path problems; *must-mention* topic in 14-mark questions.
- **Pheromone evaporation** prevents **unbounded amplification** and supports **exploration of new routes**.
- **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** — formalization by **Marco Dorigo (1992)**.
- **Transition rule**: $P_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Pheromone update**: $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Artificial ant enhancements over real ants**: memory (tabu list), discrete time, global pheromone, problem-specific heuristic.
- **Parameters to remember**: $\alpha \in [1,5]$, $\beta \in [2,5]$, $\rho \approx 0.5$, $m \approx n$ (one ant per node in TSP).
- **Exploration vs. Exploitation trade-off**: $\alpha$ → exploitation (memory), $\beta$ → exploration (heuristic).
- **Applications** (must list 4 in 14-mark questions): TSP, VRP, Network Routing (AntNet), Job-Shop Scheduling.
- **Formula derivation order in KTU exams**: start from the ODE $\frac{d\tau}{dt} = -\rho\tau$, then exponential decay, then linearization $(1-\rho)$, then add deposition.
- **Negative emergence** example: the "ant mill" — ants following pheromone in a loop until they die.
- **Stigmergy has two sub-types**: *sematectonic* (physical) and *sign-based* (chemical/light) — examiners love this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) - S-ACO, Ant Colony Optimization Metaheuristic, Combinatorial Optimization, ACO Metaheuristic Problem Solving using ACO, Local Search Methods, Scope of ACO Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAD862 - BIO INSPIRED OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) - S-ACO, Ant Colony Optimization Metaheuristic, Combinatorial Optimization, ACO Metaheuristic Problem Solving using ACO, Local Search Methods, Scope of ACO Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) — Formal Definition

**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a class of population-based, metaheuristic swarm intelligence algorithms inspired by the foraging behaviour of real ants. Formally, ACO is a probabilistic technique for solving computational problems which can be reduced to finding good paths through graphs.

The artificial ants in ACO are stochastic solution construction procedures that build solutions by moving on a **construction graph** $\mathcal{G} = (C, L, \tau)$ where:
- $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_{N_C}\}$ is a finite set of components
- $L$ is a finite set of connections (edges) between components
- $\tau$ is the **pheromone model** (set of pheromone values $\tau_{ij}$ associated with each connection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ACO was first proposed by **Marco Dorigo** in his 1992 PhD thesis. It is the canonical example of **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** where simple agents coordinate via indirect communication called **stigmergy** (communication through environment modification).

## 1.2 The Ant System (AS) — Foundation

The original **Ant System (AS)** is the ancestor of all ACO algorithms. It is a cooperative population-based algorithm that uses three mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| **Pheromone Trail ($\tau$)** | Long-term memory of good solutions (longer-term information) |
| **Heuristic Information ($\eta$)** | Problem-specific greedy information (short-term information) |
| **Pheromone Evaporation ($\rho$)** | Avoids premature convergence, enables exploration |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Ants in a Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Finding the Shortest Path to Sugar"**

Imagine a colony of ants in your kitchen trying to reach a sugar jar. Ants deposit a chemical called **pheromone** along their path. Initially, ants choose randomly between a long path and a short path to the sugar.

- **First few minutes:** Both paths get used. The short path receives pheromone deposit *faster* because ants on it complete the round trip in less time.
- **After some time:** The shorter path has a *thicker* pheromone trail. New ants preferentially follow the strong pheromone.
- **Long-term behaviour:** The shorter path dominates, but some exploration continues because pheromone evaporates.

**The "Aha!" moment:** ACO mimics this with three key behaviours — **(1) Probabilistic Path Selection**, **(2) Pheromone Reinforcement (positive feedback)**, and **(3) Pheromone Evaporation (negative feedback)**.

## 1.4 Combinatorial Optimization — The Problem Class

**Combinatorial Optimization (CO)** is the process of finding an optimal solution (minimum or maximum) from a finite (but practically enormous) set of discrete objects.

Formally, a **CO problem instance** is a triple $(S, \Omega, f)$ where:
- $S$ is the **search space** (set of candidate solutions)
- $\Omega$ is the **set of constraints**
- $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ is the **objective function** to be minimized

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **solution** $s^* \in S$ is called a **global optimum** if $f(s^*) \leq f(s) \quad \forall s \in S$. The search space is discrete (not continuous), which differentiates CO from classical continuous optimization.

### Examples of CO Problems (KTU-Exam Favourites)

| Problem | Acronym | Decision Variables |
|---------|---------|--------------------|
| **Travelling Salesman Problem** | TSP | Permutation of cities |
| **Vehicle Routing Problem** | VRP | Sequence of customer visits |
| **Quadratic Assignment Problem** | QAP | Facility-to-location assignment |
| **Job-Shop Scheduling** | JSP | Order of operations on machines |
| **Graph Coloring** | GCP | Color assignment to vertices |
| **Shortest Common Super-Sequence** | SCS | String arrangement |

## 1.5 Simple ACO (S-ACO) — The First Algorithm

**S-ACO (Simple ACO)** is the simplest instantiation of the Ant System. It uses the **ant-cycle** model of pheromone update where pheromone is deposited only after a complete solution is built.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Simple"?** S-ACO uses a single rule: *solutions that are shorter receive more pheromone.* No complex local search, no elitism — just the bare ant metaphor distilled to its essence.

The S-ACO distinguishes itself from AS by:
1. Using only **global pheromone update** (no local update during construction)
2. Pheromone is updated proportionally to **inverse tour length** $1/L_k$
3. Strictly follows the **ant-cycle** paradigm

## 1.6 ACO Metaheuristic — The General Framework

The **ACO Metaheuristic** is the *algorithmic skeleton* that abstracts all ACO variants. It consists of three procedural components executed in a loop:

```
ACO_Metaheuristic:
   1. InitializePheromoneTrails
   2. WHILE (termination condition not met):
        a. ConstructAntsSolutions   ← each ant builds a solution
        b. ApplyLocalSearch (optional) ← improve solutions
        c. UpdatePheromones        ← evaporate + deposit
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **pheromone update** is the heart of ACO. It performs two operations: **evaporation** (decay) and **deposit** (reinforcement). The ratio between them controls the **exploration vs exploitation** trade-off.

## 1.7 Local Search Methods — Hill Climbing in Disguise

A **local search** algorithm starts from an initial solution and iteratively moves to a *neighbouring* solution with better objective value. It terminates when no neighbour improves the solution (a **local optimum**).

For TSP, the most common local search operators are:

| Operator | Action | Best Use-Case |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| **2-opt** | Remove 2 edges, reconnect to reverse a sub-tour | Small/Medium TSP |
| **3-opt** | Remove 3 edges, try 7 reconnections | Large TSP |
| **Or-opt** | Move a chain of 3 cities to another location | Geometric TSP |
| **Node-swap** | Swap positions of two cities | General graphs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** ACO + Local Search = **Hybrid ACO**. Most state-of-the-art ACO implementations (ACS, MMAS) use local search to boost solution quality. The pheromone guides the search; local search refines the result.

## 1.8 Scope of ACO Algorithms

ACO has been successfully applied to over **100+ combinatorial problems**. The major application domains are:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ACO APPLICATION DOMAINS (SCOPE)                         │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  • Routing Problems: TSP, VRP, ARCP                     │
│  • Assignment: QAP, SAP, Graph Coloring                  │
│  • Scheduling: Job-shop, Flow-shop, Project scheduling   │
│  • Network Routing: Telecommunication, MANET             │
│  • Bioinformatics: Protein folding, DNA sequencing        │
│  • Machine Learning: Rule induction, clustering          │
│  • Engineering Design: Structural optimization           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ACO Algorithm — Operational Breakdown

The ACO metaheuristic operates in **three concurrent phases** during each iteration:

### Phase 1: Solution Construction (Ant Movement)

Each ant $k$ at node $i$ selects the next node $j$ using a **stochastic transition rule**. This rule balances two competing forces:
- **Pheromone trail $\tau_{ij}$** (memory of past success)
- **Heuristic desirability $\eta_{ij}$** (greedy, problem-specific information)

The parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ control the relative influence:
- $\alpha = 0$ → pure heuristic (no learning)
- $\beta = 0$ → pure pheromone (no greediness, slow convergence)

### Phase 2: Local Search (Optional Refinement)

If a local search $\mathcal{LS}$ is applied, each ant's solution $s$ is replaced by a locally optimal version $\mathcal{LS}(s)$. This is a **deterministic** step that refines the stochastic ACO output.

### Phase 3: Pheromone Update (Memory Consolidation)

Two sub-operations occur:
1. **Evaporation** — All pheromone trails decay by factor $(1-\rho)$ where $\rho \in (0,1]$ is the **evaporation rate**
2. **Deposit** — Ants deposit new pheromone proportional to solution quality

## 2.2 Key ACO Algorithms — A Comparative Theory

| Algorithm | Year | Key Innovation | Pheromone Update |
|-----------|------|----------------|------------------|
| **Ant System (AS)** | 1991 | First ACO | Ant-cycle, all ants update |
| **Elitist AS** | 1992 | Best-so-far extra deposit | $L_{bs}$ weighted |
| **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | 1997 | Local + global update | Pseudo-random + Q/L |
| **Max-Min AS (MMAS)** | 1996 | Pheromone bounds $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ | Only best ant updates |
| **Rank-based AS (AS_rank)** | 1997 | Sorted weighted update | Top $(w-1)$ ants update |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-CRITICAL FORMULAS** — Memorize these for numerical problems in Part B.

| # | Formula | Name | Description |
|---|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | $P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \, [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \, [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | **Transition Probability** | Probability ant $k$ moves from $i$ to $j$ |
| 2 | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | **Heuristic Value (TSP)** | Inverse of distance |
| 3 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$ | **Pheromone Update (AS)** | Evaporation + deposit |
| 4 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if } (i,j) \in T^{k}(t) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | **Ant-Cycle Deposit** | $Q$ = constant, $L_k$ = tour length |
| 5 | $\tau_{ij}^{ACS} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \rho \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best}$ | **ACS Global Update** | Only best ant updates |
| 6 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = \dfrac{1}{L_{best}}$ | **ACS Deposit** | $L_{best}$ = best tour |
| 7 | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\xi)\tau_{ij} + \xi \cdot \tau_{0}$ | **ACS Local Update** | During construction |
| 8 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L_{best}}$ | **MMAS Upper Bound** | Theoretical max pheromone |
| 9 | $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max} \cdot (1-\sqrt[n]{p_{best}})}{(avg-1)\sqrt[n]{p_{best}}}$ | **MMAS Lower Bound** | $p_{best}$ = prob of best tour |
| 10 | $f(s^*) = \min_{s \in S} f(s)$ | **Optimization Goal** | Global optimum |

## 2.4 Parameter Semantics — Why Each Matters

### **$\alpha$ — Pheromone Influence**
Controls how much the ant *trusts collective memory*. Higher $\alpha$ → ants follow established trails → faster convergence, higher risk of stagnation.

### **$\beta$ — Heuristic Influence**
Controls how much the ant *trusts greedy information*. Higher $\beta$ → ants prefer nearby nodes → faster but more myopic search.

### **$\rho$ — Evaporation Rate**
Higher $\rho$ → faster forgetting → more exploration. Lower $\rho$ → slower forgetting → more exploitation. Typical value: $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$.

### **$m$ — Number of Ants**
Trade-off: more ants = better coverage but slower per-iteration. Dorigo recommends $m = n$ where $n$ is the number of cities.

### **$Q$ — Pheromone Constant**
In Ant-Cycle model, $Q$ is the total pheromone deposited by an ant. Typical: $Q \in [1, 10000]$. In Ant-Density, $Q$ is per edge; in Ant-Quantity, $Q/d_{ij}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where ACO is used in industry:**
> - **Telecommunications:** Routing in packet-switched networks (AntNet)
> - **Logistics:** UPS uses OR-tools (ACO variant) for delivery routing
> - **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure prediction, RNA secondary structure
> - **Manufacturing:** Job-shop scheduling in semiconductor fabs
> - **Robotics:** Multi-robot path planning and warehouse automation

The **engineering trade-off** ACO handles beautifully: it doesn't guarantee the optimum, but it finds *very good solutions quickly* to problems where exhaustive search is impossible (e.g., TSP with 1000 cities has $10^{2567}$ routes).

## 2.6 The Pheromone Equation — Three Models

| Model | Deposit Formula | When Used |
|-------|----------------|-----------|
| **Ant-Density** | $\Delta\tau = Q$ (per edge) | Local decisions |
| **Ant-Quantity** | $\Delta\tau = Q/d_{ij}$ (per edge) | Local decisions |
| **Ant-Cycle** | $\Delta\tau = Q/L_k$ (per tour) | **Global update (preferred)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, you will almost always use the **Ant-Cycle model**. The deposit $\Delta\tau = Q/L_k$ is applied *after* a full tour is completed, not edge-by-edge.

## 2.7 Theoretical Guarantees and Properties

- **Convergence in Value:** For any $\epsilon > 0$ and sufficiently small $\rho$, the probability that an ACO algorithm finds at least one optimal solution within $t$ iterations approaches 1 as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
- **No Guarantee of Global Optimum:** ACO is a *metaheuristic* — it provides no certificate of optimality.
- **Positive Feedback Property:** Good solutions attract more ants → reinforced → even better.
- **Negative Feedback Property:** Evaporation prevents one path from dominating → diversification preserved.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Travelling Salesman Problem with 5 Cities

Let us solve a TSP instance using **S-ACO** with the **ant-cycle** model. This is a typical KTU Part-B numerical.

### **Problem Setup (Given Data)**

Five cities with Euclidean distances (in arbitrary units) given in the distance matrix $D$:

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 5 & 6 & 4 \\ 2 & 0 & 4 & 5 & 3 \\ 5 & 4 & 0 & 7 & 2 \\ 6 & 5 & 7 & 0 & 8 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 & 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**ACO Parameters:** $m = 3$ ants, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$, $\rho = 0.3$, $Q = 10$, initial pheromone $\tau_0 = 0.5$ on all edges.

**Heuristic information:** $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ (computed below).

### **Step 1: Compute Heuristic Matrix $\eta$**

For each pair $(i,j)$ where $i \neq j$, compute $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$:

$$
\eta_{12} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500, \quad \eta_{13} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200, \quad \eta_{14} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167, \quad \eta_{15} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250
$$

$$
\eta_{23} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250, \quad \eta_{24} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200, \quad \eta_{25} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
\eta_{34} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.143, \quad \eta_{35} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500, \quad \eta_{45} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125
$$

By symmetry $\eta_{ij} = \eta_{ji}$.

### **Step 2: Initialize Pheromone Matrix $\tau$**

All edges start with $\tau_0 = 0.5$:

$$
\tau^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} - & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & - & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 & - & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 & - & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 & - \end{bmatrix}
$$

### **Step 3: Construct Solutions (Ant 1, starting at City 1)**

Since initial pheromone is uniform, the transition probability simplifies to:

$$
P_{1j}^{k} = \frac{[\tau_{1j}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{1j}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_1^{k}} [\tau_{1l}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{1l}]^{\beta}}
$$

**From City 1**, Ant 1 considers cities $\{2, 3, 4, 5\}$:

Compute $[\tau]^{\alpha}[\eta]^{\beta} = (0.5)^1 \cdot (\eta_{1j})^2$:

| $j$ | $\eta_{1j}$ | $\eta_{1j}^{2}$ | $\tau \cdot \eta^{2}$ |
|-----|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 2 | 0.500 | 0.2500 | 0.1250 |
| 3 | 0.200 | 0.0400 | 0.0200 |
| 4 | 0.167 | 0.0278 | 0.0139 |
| 5 | 0.250 | 0.0625 | 0.0313 |

**Sum** $= 0.1250 + 0.0200 + 0.0139 + 0.0313 = 0.1902$

Probabilities:

$$
P_{12} = \frac{0.1250}{0.1902} \approx 0.657, \quad P_{13} = \frac{0.0200}{0.1902} \approx 0.105, \quad P_{14} = \frac{0.0139}{0.1902} \approx 0.073, \quad P_{15} = \frac{0.0313}{0.1902} \approx 0.165
$$

**Ant 1 chooses City 2** (highest probability $0.657$). Tour so far: $1 \rightarrow 2$.

### **Step 4: Continue Construction (Ant 1, from City 2)**

Remaining cities: $\{3, 4, 5\}$.

| $j$ | $\eta_{2j}$ | $\eta_{2j}^{2}$ | $\tau \cdot \eta^{2}$ |
|-----|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 3 | 0.250 | 0.0625 | 0.0313 |
| 4 | 0.200 | 0.0400 | 0.0200 |
| 5 | 0.333 | 0.1111 | 0.0556 |

**Sum** $= 0.0313 + 0.0200 + 0.0556 = 0.1069$

$$
P_{23} = \frac{0.0313}{0.1069} \approx 0.293, \quad P_{24} = \frac{0.0200}{0.1069} \approx 0.187, \quad P_{25} = \frac{0.0556}{0.1069} \approx 0.520
$$

**Ant 1 chooses City 5** (highest probability $0.520$). Tour so far: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 5$.

### **Step 5: Continue (Ant 1, from City 5)**

Remaining cities: $\{3, 4\}$.

| $j$ | $\eta_{5j}$ | $\eta_{5j}^{2}$ | $\tau \cdot \eta^{2}$ |
|-----|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 3 | 0.500 | 0.2500 | 0.1250 |
| 4 | 0.125 | 0.0156 | 0.0078 |

**Sum** $= 0.1250 + 0.0078 = 0.1328$

$$
P_{53} = \frac{0.1250}{0.1328} \approx 0.941, \quad P_{54} = \frac{0.0078}{0.1328} \approx 0.059
$$

**Ant 1 chooses City 3** (very high prob $0.941$). Tour so far: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 3$.

**Last step:** City 4 is forced. Tour: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 1$.

### **Step 6: Compute Tour Length for Ant 1**

$$
L_1 = d_{12} + d_{25} + d_{53} + d_{34} + d_{41} = 2 + 3 + 2 + 7 + 6 = 20
$$

### **Step 7: Pheromone Update (End of Iteration 1)**

Assume all 3 ants produced tours with lengths $L_1 = 20$, $L_2 = 18$, $L_3 = 22$ (hypothetical, computed similarly).

Evaporation: each existing pheromone multiplied by $(1 - 0.3) = 0.7$.

Deposit for each ant $k$ on each edge of its tour: $\Delta\tau = Q/L_k$.

For Ant 1 ($L_1 = 20$): $\Delta\tau = 10/20 = 0.5$ on edges $(1,2), (2,5), (5,3), (3,4), (4,1)$.

**Updated pheromone for edge (1,2):**

$$
\tau_{12}^{new} = (1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{12}^{old} + \sum_{k=1}^{3} \Delta\tau_{12}^{k} = 0.7 \times 0.5 + 0.5 + \Delta_2 + \Delta_3
$$

Assuming only Ant 1 visited edge (1,2):

$$
\tau_{12}^{new} = 0.35 + 0.5 = 0.85
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The pheromone on frequently used edges increases (0.5 → 0.85), while pheromone on unused edges decreases (0.5 → 0.35) due to evaporation alone. This is the core "self-organizing" behaviour of ACO.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Properties of the Pheromone Update

Let us formalize the dynamics. Define $E_{ij}(t) = 1$ if edge $(i,j)$ was traversed by *any* ant in iteration $t$, else 0.

**Pheromone Decay for Unused Edges:**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) \quad \text{(no deposit)}
$$

$$
\tau_{ij}(t) = (1-\rho)^{t} \cdot \tau_0 \quad \text{(after } t \text{ iterations unused)}
$$

This shows **exponential decay**. Unused edges eventually become "forgotten".

**Pheromone Growth for Used Edges (worst case — only one ant visits):**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \frac{Q}{L_{worst}}
$$

**Steady-State Analysis:** In equilibrium, $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) = \tau^*$:

$$
\tau^* = (1-\rho)\tau^* + \frac{Q}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho \cdot \tau^* = \frac{Q}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau^* = \frac{Q}{\rho L}
$$

This is the **theoretical equilibrium pheromone value** on a tour of length $L$ visited by one ant per iteration.

## 3.3 Convergence of the Transition Probability

If all ants consistently choose edge $(i,j)$ (the global optimum edge), we want to verify the probability converges to 1.

**Recursive expansion** for a path reinforced by $n$ ants per iteration:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{(n)} = (1-\rho)^n \tau_0 + \frac{Q}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1-\rho)^k
$$

Using the **geometric series formula** $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k = \frac{1-r^n}{1-r}$:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{(n)} = (1-\rho)^n \tau_0 + \frac{Q}{L} \cdot \frac{1 - (1-\rho)^n}{\rho}
$$

**Taking the limit as $n \rightarrow \infty$** (since $0 < (1-\rho) < 1$):

$$
\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \tau_{ij}^{(n)} = 0 + \frac{Q}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{Q}{\rho L}
$$

This confirms the **steady-state result** derived above. The pheromone converges to $\tau^* = Q/(\rho L)$, which is the **expected pheromone intensity** for a tour of length $L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The parameter $\rho$ inversely affects equilibrium pheromone. Lower $\rho$ (slower evaporation) → higher steady-state $\tau^*$ → more exploitation. Higher $\rho$ → faster forgetting → more exploration.

## 3.4 Probabilistic Action Selection — Roulette Wheel

The transition rule is a **softmax / roulette wheel selection**. The probability mass function (PMF) over the neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}_i^k$ is:

$$
P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{w_{ij}(t)}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^{k}} w_{il}(t)}, \quad \text{where } w_{ij}(t) = [\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}
$$

**Sum constraint verification:**

$$
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i^{k}} P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i^{k}} \frac{w_{ij}(t)}{\sum_{l} w_{il}(t)} = \frac{\sum_{j} w_{ij}(t)}{\sum_{l} w_{il}(t)} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

The probabilities form a valid PMF (sum to 1, all non-negative).

## 3.5 ACS Pseudo-Random Proportional Rule

Ant Colony System (ACS) uses a deterministic-greedy action selection with probability $q_0$:

$$
j = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^{k}} \{[\tau_{il}] \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}\} & \text{if } q \leq q_0 \text{ (exploitation)} \\ J & \text{otherwise (exploration, roulette wheel)} \end{cases}
$$

where $q \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$ is uniform random, and $J$ is drawn according to the AS transition rule.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: ACO Metaheuristic High-Level Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Pheromone] --> B[Set t = 0]
    B --> C{t < Max_Iterations?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Place m Ants on Random Nodes]
    D --> E[Each Ant Builds Solution Step by Step]
    E --> F{Local Search Enabled?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Local Search to Each Tour]
    F -- No --> H[Skip Local Search]
    G --> I[Compute Tour Length L_k for Each Ant]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Identify Best Tour L_best]
    J --> K[Pheromone Evaporation: Multiply by 1-rho]
    K --> L[Pheromone Deposit on Visited Edges]
    L --> M[Update t = t + 1]
    M --> C
    C -- No --> N[Output Best Solution Found]
    N --> O[End]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

        B2[Heuristic Engine eta]

        B4[Pheromone Manager]
    end

    end

    B1 -- read --> B4
    B1 --> B3

    B1 --> C1

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Ant Solution Construction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PHER as Pheromone Matrix
    participant HEUR as Heuristic Matrix
    participant ANT as Artificial Ant k
    participant TOUR as Solution Tour

    Note over ANT: Start at random city i_0
    ANT->>PHER: Read tau_ij for all j in N
    ANT->>HEUR: Read eta_ij for all j in N
    ANT->>ANT: Compute w_ij = tau^alpha * eta^beta
    ANT->>ANT: Normalize to get P_ij
    ANT->>ANT: Select j via roulette wheel
    ANT->>TOUR: Append edge i -> j
    ANT->>PHER: Apply ACS local update tau_ij
    Note over ANT: Repeat until all cities visited
    ANT->>TOUR: Close tour by returning to start
    ANT->>TOUR: Compute length L_k
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D --> E{Best Ant Type?}
    E -- AS --> F["All ants deposit: Delta tau = Q / L_k"]
    E -- ACS --> G["Best ant only: Delta tau = Q / L_best"]

    F --> I[Step 3: Apply MMAS Bounds]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Clamp: tau_min <= tau_ij <= tau_max"]
    J --> K[Updated Pheromone Matrix]
    K --> L[Ready for Next Iteration]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the concept of stigmergy in Ant Colony Optimization. How does it enable indirect communication between artificial ants?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Stigmergy** is a mechanism of indirect coordination between agents (ants) achieved through modifications of the environment, rather than through direct message-passing. In ACO, stigmergy is implemented via **pheromone trails** deposited on edges of the construction graph.

When an artificial ant builds a solution, it deposits pheromone on the edges it traverses. This pheromone acts as a *modification* of the environment that subsequent ants can sense. A higher pheromone level increases the probability of an edge being chosen by future ants, creating a **positive feedback loop**.

This is the *only* communication channel between ants in ACO — there is no explicit message exchange. The pheromone acts as a shared "scratchpad" of collective experience, allowing the colony to coordinate complex behaviours (like finding shortest paths) from simple local rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key points for 3 marks:** (1) Definition of stigmergy (1 mark), (2) Role of pheromone as environmental modification (1 mark), (3) Positive feedback mechanism enabling indirect communication (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Combinatorial Optimization (CO) and Continuous Optimization with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Combinatorial Optimization | Continuous Optimization |
|--------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Search Space** | Discrete and finite | Continuous (real-valued intervals) |
| **Decision Variables** | Integer, binary, permutations | Real numbers $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Methodology** | Metaheuristics (ACO, GA, TS) | Calculus-based (gradients), evolutionary |
| **Examples** | TSP, Knapsack, Graph Coloring, QAP | Function minimization, Neural net training |
| **Local Optima** | Many (combinatorial explosion) | Fewer (smooth landscape) |
| **Guaranteed Methods** | Exhaustive search (infeasible) | Newton's method, KKT conditions |

**Examples:**
- **CO:** TSP (find shortest tour of 10 cities — $10! = 3,628,800$ routes)
- **Continuous:** Minimize $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6$ over $x \in \mathbb{R}$

The fundamental difference: CO problems are defined on **discrete structures** (graphs, sets, sequences), while continuous optimization is defined on **real vector spaces**.
