topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAD862 - BIO INSPIRED OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies - ACO and PSO for NP-hard Problems, Routing Problems, Assignment Problems, Scheduling Problems, Subset Problems, Machine Learning Problems, Travelling Salesman Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 NP-Hard Problems and Metaheuristic Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **NP-Hard Problem** is a class of decision/optimization problems for which no known polynomial-time deterministic algorithm exists. The search space grows **combinatorially** (often exponentially) with problem size, making exhaustive enumeration computationally infeasible for real-world instances.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner's Wording):**
> A problem $H$ is NP-hard if every problem $L \in \text{NP}$ can be reduced to $H$ in polynomial time. NP-hard problems need **not** belong to NP themselves. They are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP.

### 1.1.1 Intuition: Why Classical Methods Fail

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Lost Keys in a Stadium"**
> Imagine you dropped your keys somewhere in a 100,000-seat stadium at night. A *deterministic* search (exhaustive grid scan) would force you to check every seat. A *metaheuristic* (ACO/PSO) acts like a smart, communicating crowd: each "agent" explores a region, deposits clues (pheromones) or shares GPS coordinates (swarm best positions), and the **collective intelligence** converges toward the likely drop zone in minutes. You trade the guarantee of finding the *exact* key for the *practical certainty* of finding a "good enough" key fast.

### 1.1.2 Why ACO and PSO for NP-Hard Problems?

| Property | ACO | PSO |
|---|---|---|
| **Inspiration** | Ant foraging behavior | Bird flocking / fish schooling |
| **Solution Representation** | Probabilistic constructive graph | Continuous/swarm position vectors |
| **Memory Mechanism** | Pheromone matrix $\tau_{ij}$ | $pbest_i$, $gbest$ |
| **Best Suited For** | Discrete combinatorial problems | Continuous & discrete (with binary PSO) |
| **Parallelism** | Colony-based, naturally parallel | Swarm-based, vectorizable |
| **Convergence Speed** | Moderate, high solution quality | Fast, sometimes premature convergence |

---

## 1.2 Taxonomy of NP-Hard Problems Covered in KTU Module 4

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Highlighted Problem Domains:**
> 1. **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — discrete routing
> 2. **Routing Problems (VRP, Network Routing)** — capacitated graph search
> 3. **Assignment Problems (QAP)** — combinatorial matching
> 4. **Scheduling Problems (JSSP, FSSP)** — permutation ordering
> 5. **Subset Problems (Knapsack, Set Cover)** — 0/1 selection
> 6. **Machine Learning Problems** — feature selection, NN training, clustering

### 1.2.1 The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) — The Canonical NP-Hard Benchmark

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Given a set of $n$ cities with pairwise distances $d_{ij}$, find the shortest **Hamiltonian cycle** that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting city. TSP is NP-hard; its decision variant is NP-complete.

**Search Space Size:** $(n-1)!/2$ for symmetric TSP.

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy for Each Problem

> [!TIP]
> - **TSP** → A delivery driver planning the most efficient daily route among 50 customers.
> - **VRP** → A fleet of trucks with capacity limits serving customers from a central depot.
> - **QAP** → Assigning $n$ departments to $n$ buildings such that interaction flow is maximized and distance costs are minimized.
> - **JSSP** → Sequencing $n$ jobs on $m$ machines, each with a specific operation order, to minimize makespan.
> - **Knapsack** → Packing a backpack of capacity $W$ with the most valuable subset of items.
> - **ML Feature Selection** → Choosing the best subset of features that maximizes model accuracy.

### 1.2.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Board Emphasis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold-Listed Standard Metrics for Evaluation:**
> - **Best Known Solution (BKS)** quality
> - **Relative Percentage Deviation (RPD):** $\text{RPD} = \frac{|f_{\text{algo}} - f_{\text{BKS}}|}{f_{\text{BKS}}} \times 100\%$
> - **CPU Time / Wall-clock Time** in seconds
> - **Number of Function Evaluations (NFE)**
> - **Convergence Iteration** at which the algorithm stabilizes

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) — Algorithmic Blueprint

### 2.1.1 Operational Steps (Structured Logic)

1. **Initialization:** Place $m$ ants on $n$ cities/nodes; initialize pheromone matrix $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0$ (small positive constant).
2. **Solution Construction:** Each ant $k$ probabilistically builds a solution by selecting next node $j$ from allowed set $\mathcal{N}_i^k$.
3. **Local Pheromone Update (optional):** Apply evaporation to visited edges.
4. **Solution Evaluation:** Compute tour cost $L_k$ for each ant.
5. **Global Pheromone Update:** Deposit pheromone on edges belonging to the best solutions; evaporate all edges.
6. **Daemon Actions (optional):** Apply local search (e.g., 2-opt) to best solution.
7. **Termination Check:** Stop if $t = t_{\max}$ or stagnation detected.

### 2.1.2 Core ACO Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition Probability (State Transition Rule):**
> $$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}, \quad \text{if } j \in \mathcal{N}_i^k$$
> where $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ is the **heuristic visibility**, $\alpha$ controls pheromone influence, $\beta$ controls heuristic influence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Pheromone Update Rule (AS — Ant System):**
> $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$$
> $$\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses edge } (i,j) \text{ in its tour} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
> with evaporation rate $\rho \in (0,1)$, $Q$ = pheromone constant, $L_k$ = tour length of ant $k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **ACS (Ant Colony System) Variants (commonly asked):**
> - **ACS Transition Rule:** Pseudo-random proportional balance between exploitation and exploration using $q_0$.
> - **ACS Global Update:** Only the best-so-far ant deposits pheromone:
>   $$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \rho \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bs}$$
> - **MMAS (Max-Min Ant System):** Pheromone bounds $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ prevent stagnation.

---

## 2.2 Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) — Algorithmic Blueprint

### 2.2.1 Operational Steps (Structured Logic)

1. **Initialization:** Randomly initialize positions $x_i(0)$ and velocities $v_i(0)$ for $N$ particles within bounds.
2. **Fitness Evaluation:** Compute $f(x_i)$ for each particle.
3. **Update Personal Best:** $pbest_i \leftarrow x_i$ if $f(x_i) < f(pbest_i)$.
4. **Update Global Best:** $gbest \leftarrow \arg\min_i f(pbest_i)$.
5. **Velocity Update:** Apply inertia + cognitive + social components.
6. **Position Update:** Move particle to new position.
7. **Boundary Handling:** Clamp or reflect if out of bounds.
8. **Termination Check:** Stop if $t = t_{\max}$ or convergence criterion met.

### 2.2.2 Core PSO Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Update Equation (Inertia-Weight PSO):**
> $$v_i^d(t+1) = w \cdot v_i^d(t) + c_1 r_1 \bigl(pbest_i^d - x_i^d(t)\bigr) + c_2 r_2 \bigl(gbest^d - x_i^d(t)\bigr)$$
> where $w$ = inertia weight, $c_1$ = cognitive coefficient, $c_2$ = social coefficient, $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Position Update Equation:**
> $$x_i^d(t+1) = x_i^d(t) + v_i^d(t+1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clerc-Kennedy Constriction Coefficient Variant:**
> $$\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$$
> Used to ensure convergence without velocity clamping.

### 2.2.3 Binary PSO (BPSO) for Discrete Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sigmoid Mapping for Discrete/Binary Spaces:**
> $$v_i^d(t+1) = w v_i^d(t) + c_1 r_1(pbest_i^d - x_i^d) + c_2 r_2(gbest^d - x_i^d)$$
> $$S(v_i^d) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v_i^d}}$$
> $$x_i^d(t+1) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{rand}() < S(v_i^d) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
> Used for **knapsack, feature selection, subset selection**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbols | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ACO transition prob. | $p_{ij}^k = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum [\tau_{il}]^\alpha [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$ | TSP, VRP, QAP |
| 2 | ACO pheromone update | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$ | All ACO |
| 3 | $\Delta\tau$ AS model | $Q/L_k$ | Ant System |
| 4 | $\Delta\tau$ ACS model | $Q/L_{bs}$ (best-so-far) | ACS |
| 5 | MMAS bounds | $\tau_{\min} \leq \tau_{ij} \leq \tau_{\max}$ | Avoid stagnation |
| 6 | PSO velocity | $v_{i}^{d} = w v_i^d + c_1 r_1(pbest - x) + c_2 r_2(gbest - x)$ | All PSO |
| 7 | PSO position | $x_i^d(t+1) = x_i^d(t) + v_i^d(t+1)$ | All PSO |
| 8 | Constriction $\chi$ | $\chi = 2 / \vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi(\phi-4)} \vert$ | Stable PSO |
| 9 | BPSO sigmoid | $S(v) = 1/(1+e^{-v})$ | Binary/discrete |
| 10 | RPD metric | $\text{RPD} = \vert f_{\text{algo}} - f_{\text{BKS}} \vert / f_{\text{BKS}} \times 100$ | Benchmarking |
| 11 | TSP search space | $(n-1)!/2$ | Complexity |
| 12 | Knapsack constraint | $\sum w_i x_i \leq W, \, x_i \in \{0,1\}$ | Subset |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **ACO for TSP** → VLSI circuit drilling, DNA sequencing, telescope scheduling.
> - **PSO for Neural Networks** → Training feedforward/RBF networks without gradient.
> - **ACO for VRP** → Amazon/Flipkart last-mile delivery.
> - **PSO for Job-Shop Scheduling** → Boeing/Airbus production lines.
> - **BPSO for Feature Selection** → Gene selection in bioinformatics, credit scoring.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Case Study 1: ACO for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU Board Style):**
> Consider an asymmetric TSP with 4 cities: A, B, C, D. The distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$ is given below. Apply **Ant System (AS)** for **2 iterations** with $m = 3$ ants, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$, $\rho = 0.5$, $Q = 1$. Initial pheromone $\tau_0 = 1$ on all edges.

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 2 & 9 & 10 \\ 6 & \infty & 3 & 7 \\ 8 & 4 & \infty & 5 \\ 1 & 12 & 6 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Initialize Heuristic Visibility

$$
\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}
$$

$$
\eta = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.5 & 0.111 & 0.1 \\ 0.167 & 0 & 0.333 & 0.143 \\ 0.125 & 0.25 & 0 & 0.2 \\ 1.0 & 0.083 & 0.167 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Iteration 1, Ant 1 starts at City A

**Ant 1 at City A. Allowed set: {B, C, D}.**

$$
P_{AB} = \frac{\tau_{AB}^\alpha \cdot \eta_{AB}^\beta}{\sum_l \tau_{Al}^\alpha \eta_{Al}^\beta} = \frac{(1)^1 (0.5)^2}{(1)(0.5)^2 + (1)(0.111)^2 + (1)(0.1)^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.25}{0.25 + 0.0123 + 0.01} = \frac{0.25}{0.2723} \approx 0.918
$$

$$
P_{AC} \approx \frac{0.0123}{0.2723} \approx 0.045, \quad P_{AD} \approx \frac{0.01}{0.2723} \approx 0.037
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic:** Heuristic is raised to $\beta = 2$, so high-visibility edges dominate. The strong $\eta_{AB} = 0.5$ wins → Ant 1 moves to **B**.

**Ant 1 path so far: A → B. Allowed: {C, D}.**

$$
P_{BC} = \frac{(1)(0.333)^2}{(1)(0.333)^2 + (1)(0.143)^2} = \frac{0.111}{0.111 + 0.0204} \approx 0.845
$$

$$
P_{BD} \approx 0.155
$$

Ant 1 moves to **C**. Path: A → B → C → (must visit D) → return to A.

**Ant 1's complete tour:** A → B → C → D → A

Tour length $L_1 = d_{AB} + d_{BC} + d_{CD} + d_{DA} = 2 + 3 + 5 + 1 = \mathbf{11}$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Iter 1, Pheromone Update (AS rule)

Assume for brevity that all 3 ants produce tours with computed lengths $L_1, L_2, L_3$. Global update:

$$
\tau_{ij}(1) = (1 - 0.5)(1) + \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{1}{L_k} = 0.5 + \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{1}{L_k}
$$

For each visited edge $(i,j)$ of ant $k$, $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = 1/L_k$. Edges not in any tour get $0.5$ (evaporation residue).

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Iteration 2 uses updated $\tau_{ij}(1)$

Ants re-select paths with updated pheromones, eventually converging to optimal or near-optimal tour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected Output for the 4-city ATSP:** Best tour length approaches the optimum of **11** (A→B→C→D→A) after several iterations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Case Study 2: PSO for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) — Permutation Encoding

> [!NOTE]
> **Permutation-PSO Approach (KTU Board Style):**
> Each particle's position represents a city permutation. Velocity is defined as a **swap sequence** (list of swap operations).

### 3.2.1 Representation

A particle $i$ has position $X_i = (\pi_i)$ = permutation of cities, e.g., $X_i = (3, 1, 4, 2)$.

Velocity $V_i$ is a sequence of swap operations: e.g., $V_i = \{(1,3), (2,4)\}$ means swap positions 1↔3, then 2↔4.

### 3.2.2 Pseudo-Step Update

$$
X_i(t+1) = X_i(t) \oplus V_i(t+1)
$$

where $\oplus$ applies swap sequence $V_i$ to permutation $X_i$. The subtraction and addition operators are redefined as swap operations on permutations.

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic:** Unlike continuous PSO, permutation-PSO requires custom-defined "arithmetic" on permutations. Common operators: **swap**, **insertion**, **inversion**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Case Study 3: PSO for the 0/1 Knapsack Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A knapsack with capacity $W = 25$. Five items with weights $w = (12, 7, 11, 8, 9)$ and values $v = (24, 13, 23, 15, 16)$. Use **Binary PSO** with $N = 5$ particles, $c_1 = c_2 = 2$, $w = 0.7$.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Particle Initialization

Initialize positions $x_i$ as binary vectors of length 5 and velocities $v_i \in [-v_{\max}, v_{\max}]$.

Example particles:

$$
x_1 = (1, 0, 1, 0, 0), \quad v_1 = (0.3, -0.5, 0.7, 0.2, -0.4)
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Fitness with Penalty

$$
f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{5} v_i x_i - \lambda \cdot \max\!\left(0, \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i x_i - W\right)
$$

For $x_1 = (1, 0, 1, 0, 0)$:

$$
\text{Value} = 24 + 23 = 47, \quad \text{Weight} = 12 + 11 = 23 \leq 25
$$

$$
f(x_1) = 47 \quad (\text{no penalty})
$$

For $x_2 = (1, 1, 1, 1, 0)$:

$$
\text{Value} = 24 + 13 + 23 + 15 = 75, \quad \text{Weight} = 12 + 7 + 11 + 8 = 38
$$

$$
f(x_2) = 75 - \lambda (38 - 25) = 75 - 13\lambda
$$

With $\lambda = 2$: $f(x_2) = 75 - 26 = 49$.

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Velocity Update

$$
v_1^d(t+1) = 0.7 \cdot 0.3 + 2 r_1 (1 - 1) + 2 r_2 (1 - 1) = 0.21
$$

(assuming $pbest = gbest = (1, 0, 1, 0, 0)$ for position 1).

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Sigmoid Mapping

$$
S(0.21) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.21}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.811} \approx 0.552
$$

If $\text{rand}() = 0.4 < 0.552$, then $x_1^1(t+1) = 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Optimal Solution:** Knapsack capacity 25, best subset: items {1, 3, 4} with weight $12+11+8 = 31$ → infeasible. Try {1, 3, 5}: weight $12+11+9 = 32$ → infeasible. Try {2, 3, 4, 5}: weight $7+11+8+9 = 35$ → infeasible. Try {1, 2, 4, 5}: weight $12+7+8+9 = 36$ → infeasible. Try {1, 2, 3}: weight $12+7+11 = 30$ → infeasible. Try {1, 4, 5}: weight $12+8+9 = 29$ → infeasible. **Best feasible:** {1, 3} with weight 23, value 47. Alternatively {2, 3, 4}: weight 26 → infeasible. **{1, 2, 5}: weight 28 → infeasible. {2, 3}: weight 18, value 36. {3, 4, 5}: weight 28 → infeasible.**
>
> **Feasible optimum = 47** (items 1 and 3).

---

## 3.4 Worked Case Study 4: PSO for Neural Network Training (XOR Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A 2-2-1 feedforward network solves XOR. Use PSO to optimize 9 weights (2 inputs × 2 hidden + 2 hidden biases + 2 hidden × 1 output + 1 output bias = 9 weights).

### 3.4.1 Fitness Function

$$
f(W) = \sum_{p=1}^{4} (y_p - \hat{y}_p)^2 \quad \text{(Sum of Squared Errors over 4 XOR patterns)}
$$

### 3.4.2 Particle Encoding

Each particle = 9-dimensional real vector $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_9)$. Bounds: $w_i \in [-5, 5]$.

### 3.4.3 Update Mechanism

Standard continuous PSO update equations (Section 2.2.2) apply directly. PSO searches the 9-D weight space, navigating gradient-free using only fitness evaluations.

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic:** Unlike backpropagation, PSO requires no derivative computation, making it suitable for non-differentiable activation functions, noisy fitness landscapes, and multimodal error surfaces.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use ACO vs PSO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Frequently Asked Comparison Criteria:**

| Criterion | Choose ACO | Choose PSO |
|---|---|---|
| Problem is graph-based (edges/nodes) | ✓ | ✗ |
| Continuous variables with smooth landscape | ✗ | ✓ |
| Binary/discrete 0/1 decisions | ✗ | ✓ (BPSO) |
| Permutation ordering (TSP, JSSP) | ✓ (native) | ✓ (with special operators) |
| Need constructive solution building | ✓ | ✗ |
| Need fast convergence | ✗ | ✓ |
| Population diversity critical | ✓ (MMAS) | ✗ (can stagnate) |
| Hybridizable with local search | ✓ (LKH+ACO) | ✓ (PSO+SA) |
| Real-time / online applications | ✗ | ✓ |

---

## 3.6 KTU 2024 Numerical Style Practice (RPD Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Exam Numerical:**
> An ACO algorithm finds a TSP tour of length **1285** on a benchmark instance. The Best Known Solution (BKS) is **1180**. Compute the RPD.

$$
\text{RPD} = \frac{\vert f_{\text{algo}} - f_{\text{BKS}} \vert}{f_{\text{BKS}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{\vert 1285 - 1180 \vert}{1180} \times 100 = \frac{105}{1180} \times 100 \approx 8.90\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic:** Lower RPD is better. BKS acts as a normalizing reference. A value of 0% means the algorithm matched the BKS exactly.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Generic ACO Workflow for NP-Hard Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Definition] --> B[Initialize Pheromone Matrix tau_ij = tau_0]
    B --> C[Place m Ants on Random Nodes]
    C --> D{For Each Ant k}
    D --> E[Compute Probabilities p_ij using tau and eta]
    E --> F[Select Next Node Probabilistically]
    F --> G{All Nodes Visited?}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Tour Cost L_k]
    H --> I[Apply Global Pheromone Update]
    I --> J[Optional: Local Search 2-opt]
    J --> K{Termination Criterion Met?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Output Best Tour]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Generic PSO Workflow for NP-Hard Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Objective Function f x] --> B[Initialize N Particles Position and Velocity]
    B --> C[Evaluate Fitness f x_i for All Particles]
    C --> D[Update pbest_i for Each Particle]
    D --> E[Update gbest from All pbest]
    E --> F[Update Velocity v_i using pbest and gbest]
    F --> G[Update Position x_i = x_i + v_i]
    G --> H[Apply Boundary Handling Clamping or Reflection]
    H --> I{Termination Criterion Met?}
    I -- No --> C
    I -- Yes --> J[Output gbest as Best Solution]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Mapping of Problem Domains to ACO/PSO

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NP_Hard[NP-Hard Problem Domains]
        P1[TSP]
        P2[VRP Routing]
        P3[QAP Assignment]
        P4[JSSP FSSP Scheduling]
        P5[Knapsack Subset]
        P6[ML Feature Selection]
        P7[Neural Network Training]
        P8[Clustering]
    end
    subgraph Algorithms
        ACO[Ant Colony Optimization]
        PSO[Particle Swarm Optimization]
        BPSO[Binary PSO]
    end
    P1 --> ACO
    P1 --> PSO
    P2 --> ACO
    P3 --> ACO
    P4 --> ACO
    P4 --> PSO
    P5 --> BPSO
    P6 --> BPSO
    P7 --> PSO
    P8 --> PSO
    P8 --> ACO
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — ACO for TSP Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Input Cities and Distance Matrix] --> InitP[Initialize Pheromone tau_ij = tau_0]
    InitP --> Place[Place Ants on Random Cities]
    Place --> Construct[Construct Tours Probabilistically]
    Construct --> Eval[Evaluate Tour Lengths L_k]
    Eval --> Evap[Pheromone Evaporation: tau_ij times 1 minus rho]
    Evap --> Deposit[Deposit Pheromone on Best Edges: Q by L_k]
    Deposit --> IterCheck{Iteration less than t_max}
    IterCheck -- Yes --> Place
    IterCheck -- No --> Output[Output Best Hamiltonian Tour]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — PSO Convergence Behavior Across NP-Hard Problems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuous Problems] --> A1[Smooth Convergence with PSO]
    B[Discrete Permutation TSP] --> B1[Slower but High Quality with ACO]
    C[Binary Subset Knapsack] --> C1[Fast BPSO Convergence]
    D[Multi-Modal ML Problems] --> D1[PSO with Restarts for Diversity]
    E[Large-Scale VRP] --> E1[ACO with MMAS for Stability]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Hybridization Pattern (ACO + Local Search)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ACO Generates Candidate Solutions] --> B[Apply 2-opt Local Search]
    B --> C[Improved Candidate Solutions]
    C --> D[Update Pheromone with Improved Solutions Only]
    D --> E[Iterate to Convergence]
    E --> F[Output Near-Optimal Solution]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q1.** Define an **NP-hard problem**. Why are bio-inspired algorithms like ACO and PSO preferred over exact methods for solving NP-hard problems?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** An NP-hard problem is one for which no known polynomial-time algorithm exists; the search space grows combinatorially (often exponentially) with problem size, e.g., TSP has $(n-1)!/2$ possible tours.
- (1 mark) **Limitation of exact methods:** Methods like branch-and-bound, dynamic programming become computationally infeasible for $n > 30$ (TSP) or large problem instances.
- (1 mark) **Why ACO/PSO:** They are **metaheuristics** offering near-optimal solutions in polynomial time using population-based stochastic search; they exploit collective intelligence (pheromone trails in ACO, swarm memory in PSO) to escape local optima and scale to large instances.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** and **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** in terms of their inspiration, memory mechanism, and best-suited problem type.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ACO | PSO |
|---|---|---|
| (1 mark) Inspiration | Ant foraging behavior with pheromone trails | Bird flocking / fish schooling |
| (1 mark) Memory | Pheromone matrix $\tau_{ij}$ (shared, persistent) | $pbest_i$ (individual), $gbest$ (global) |
| (1 mark) Best-suited | Discrete graph problems (TSP, VRP, QAP, JSSP) | Continuous and binary problems (NN training, feature selection, knapsack) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q A (a)** Explain in detail the application of **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** to the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**. Clearly state the transition probability rule, the pheromone update rule, and the role of parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\rho$. **(7 Marks)**

> **Q A (b)** With a suitable example, explain how **PSO** can be used to solve the **0/1 Knapsack Problem** using Binary PSO. Discuss the role of the sigmoid function and penalty function approach for handling constraints. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**1. Problem Mapping (1 mark):**
The TSP is mapped to a graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = n$ cities and $|E|$ edges weighted by distance $d_{ij}$. Each ant constructs a Hamiltonian cycle.

**2. Transition Probability Rule (2 marks):**

$$
p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^\alpha [\eta_{il}]^\beta}
$$

- $\tau_{ij}$: pheromone on edge $(i,j)$ — accumulated memory
- $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$: heuristic visibility — inverse distance
- $\alpha$: weight of pheromone (long-term learning)
- $\beta$: weight of heuristic (greedy local choice)
- $\mathcal{N}_i^k$: set of cities not yet visited by ant $k$

**3. Pheromone Update Rule (2 marks):**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^k, \quad \Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \frac{Q}{L_k} \text{ if } k \text{ uses } (i,j)
$$

- $\rho$: evaporation rate (forgets bad edges, prevents stagnation)
- $Q$: pheromone deposit constant
- $L_k$: tour length of ant $k$

**4. Role of Parameters (1 mark):**
- High $\alpha$ → exploit known good edges (risk: premature convergence)
- High $\beta$ → greedy shortest-edge choice (risk: local optima)
- High $\rho$ → fast forgetting (good for dynamic problems)

**5. Algorithm Flow (1 mark):** Initialize → construct → evaluate → evaporate → deposit → iterate → output best tour.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**1. Problem Mapping (1 mark):**
The 0/1 knapsack with $n$ items, weight limit $W$, values $v_i$, weights $w_i$ is a binary optimization:
$$
\max \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \leq W, \; x_i \in \{0,1\}
$$

**2. BPSO Encoding (1.5 marks):**
Each particle is a binary vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ where $x_i = 1$ means item $i$ is selected. Velocity is continuous.

**3. Sigmoid Function (1.5 marks):**

$$
S(v_i^d) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v_i^d}}
$$

This maps velocity to a probability of setting $x_i^d = 1$. Higher velocity → higher probability of inclusion. Decision: $x_i^d = 1$ if $\text{rand}() < S(v_i^d)$, else 0.

**4. Penalty Function (1.5 marks):**
To handle infeasible solutions exceeding capacity $W$, fitness is penalized:
$$
f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i - \lambda \cdot \max\!\left(0, \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i - W\right)
$$
where $\lambda$ is a large penalty coefficient (e.g., $\lambda = 10 \cdot \max(v_i)$).

**5. Example (1.5 marks):** Worked-out example with $n=5$, $W=25$ showing particle update, sigmoid mapping, and convergence to best feasible subset (e.g., $\{1,3\}$ with value 47).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q B (a)** Discuss the application of **PSO** to the **Job-Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP)**. Explain the particle encoding, fitness function (makespan), and how velocity/position updates are adapted for permutation-based scheduling. **(7 Marks)**

> **Q B (b)** Explain the application of **ACO** to the **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)**. Discuss the construction graph, pheromone update, and the difference between TSP and VRP from an algorithmic perspective. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**1. JSSP Definition (1 mark):**
Schedule $n$ jobs on $m$ machines, where each job $j$ has a predefined operation sequence. Goal: minimize **makespan** $C_{\max} = \max_j C_j$ (completion time of last job).

**2. Particle Encoding (2 marks):**
- **Operation-based encoding:** Particle position = sequence of $n \times m$ operations, each labeled by job ID. E.g., for 3 jobs × 3 machines: $(1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3)$ — job 1's first operation, then job 2's first, etc.
- **Priority-rule encoding:** Real-valued priorities, decoded into schedule by greedy dispatch rule.

**3. Fitness Function (1 mark):**
$$
f(\pi) = C_{\max}(\pi) \quad \text{(makespan of schedule } \pi\text{)}
$$

**4. Permutation-PSO Updates (2 marks):**
Since positions are permutations, define custom operators:
- **Subtraction $X - Y$:** Set of swaps to transform $Y$ to $X$.
- **Addition $X + V$:** Apply swap sequence $V$ to permutation $X$.
- **Multiplication $\omega V$:** Probabilistically retain each swap in $V$ with probability $\omega$.

**5. Decoding & Evaluation (1 mark):**
Simulate the decoded schedule on machines, compute completion time, update $pbest$ and $gbest$.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**1. VRP Definition (1 mark):**
Given a depot, $n$ customers with demands $q_i$, and $K$ vehicles each of capacity $Q$, find routes starting/ending at the depot that serve all customers with minimum total distance, subject to $\sum_{i \in \text{route}} q_i \leq Q$.

**2. Construction Graph for ACO (2 marks):**
- **Nodes:** Depot + $n$ customers.
- **Edges:** Possible transitions.
- **Pheromone $\tau_{ij}$:** Memory of good vehicle-customer assignments.
- **Heuristic $\eta_{ij}$:** Often inverse distance or inverse demand ratio: $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.

**3. Modified Transition Rule (1.5 marks):**
VRP adds a **capacity check** to the allowed set $\mathcal{N}_i^k$:
$$
\mathcal{N}_i^k = \{j : j \text{ not visited, } q_j + \text{load}_k \leq Q\}
$$

**4. Pheromone Update (1.5 marks):**
Only edges belonging to the **best vehicle tours** receive deposits:
$$
\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \rho \cdot \frac{Q_{\text{global}}}{L_{\text{best}}}
$$

**5. TSP vs VRP Algorithmic Differences (1 mark):**
- TSP: single tour; VRP: multiple capacity-constrained tours.
- VRP introduces **splitting decisions** (when to return to depot) and **vehicle assignment** (which customer served by which vehicle).
- Pheromone structure in VRP is more complex; **depot-customer** and **customer-customer** pheromones may be modeled separately.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
> - **Pitfall 1 (ACO):** Students often forget to **normalize** the transition probability denominator across the allowed set. Always include the full summation $\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k}$.
> - **Pitfall 2 (PSO):** Confusing $pbest$ and $gbest$ — $pbest_i$ is *per-particle*; $gbest$ is *global* across the swarm. Updating $gbest$ from $pbest$ is a one-line step, not iterative.
> - **Pitfall 3 (BPSO):** Sigmoid function maps velocity to **probability** — the actual binary decision is made by comparing with a uniform random number. Do NOT confuse $S(v)$ with the final bit value.
> - **Pitfall 4 (Knapsack):** Failing to apply a **penalty function** for infeasible solutions → algorithm converges to overweight knapsacks with high nominal value.
> - **Pitfall 5 (Permutation PSO):** Standard PSO equations **do not apply** to permutation problems — must redefine $+$ and $-$ as swap-sequence operations.
> - **Pitfall 6 (RPD):** RPD denominator is **$f_{\text{BKS}}$** (the Best Known Solution), not $f_{\text{algo}}$. Many students invert this.
> - **Pitfall 7 (MMAS):** Students forget pheromone bounds $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ which are essential to prevent stagnation — a frequent 2-mark loss in ESE.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4):**

- **NP-hard** = no polynomial-time solution; search space grows combinatorially.
- **ACO** is best for **discrete graph-based combinatorial** problems (TSP, VRP, QAP, JSSP).
- **PSO** is best for **continuous and binary** problems (NN training, feature selection, knapsack).
- **ACO transition prob:** $p_{ij}^k = [\tau_{ij}]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta / \sum_l [\tau_{il}]^\alpha [\eta_{il}]^\beta$.
- **ACO pheromone update:** $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$.
- **PSO velocity:** $v_i^d = w v_i^d + c_1 r_1(pbest - x) + c_2 r_2(gbest - x)$.
- **PSO position:** $x_i^d(t+1) = x_i^d(t) + v_i^d(t+1)$.
- **BPSO sigmoid:** $S(v) = 1/(1 + e^{-v})$; sample uniform random to decide bit.
- **Constriction $\chi$** ensures stable PSO convergence when $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$.
- **MMAS** uses pheromone bounds $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ to prevent stagnation.
- **TSP** search space = $(n-1)!/2$ for symmetric case.
- **VRP** adds capacity constraints to TSP — multiple tours, depot returns.
- **QAP** assigns $n$ facilities to $n$ locations minimizing flow × distance.
- **JSSP/FSSP** schedules jobs on machines; PSO needs permutation operators.
- **Knapsack** uses Binary PSO with penalty function for capacity violation.
- **RPD** = $|f_{\text{algo}} - f_{\text{BKS}}| / f_{\text{BKS}} \times 100\%$.
- **ACO + local search** (2-opt, 3-opt) is the standard hybrid for TSP excellence.
- **Feature selection** is a binary mask problem — BPSO natively suited.
- **NN training** by PSO: particle = weight vector; fitness = SSE; gradient-free.
- **Hybrid ACO-PSO** frameworks exist but are not in KTU 2024 syllabus.
- **Performance metrics to remember:** Best, Mean, Worst, Std-Dev, RPD, NFE, CPU time, convergence iteration.
